Journal Article•[10.1016/J.BONE.2003.10.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/J.BONE.2003.10.001)

Fracture incidence and association with bone mineral density in elderly men and women: the Rotterdam Study.
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Stephanie C. E. Schuit,M. van der Klift,Angelique E. A. M. Weel,C De Laet,Huibert Burger,Ego Seeman,Albert Hofman,A. G. Uitterlinden,J.P.T.M. van Leeuwen,Huibert A. P. Pols +9 moreErasmus University Rotterdam,University Medical Center Utrecht,University of Melbourne

- 01 Jan 2004

- Bone

Show Less

TL;DR: There is a clear need for the development of more sensitive risk assessment tools, using not only BMD, but also other clinical predictors of fractures.

Journal Article•[10.1056/NEJMOA070972](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMOA070972)

Incidental findings on brain MRI in the general population.
-----------------------------------------------------------

Meike W. Vernooij,M. Arfan Ikram,Hervé L. J. Tanghe,Arnaud J P E Vincent,Albert Hofman,Gabriel P. Krestin,Wiro J. Niessen,Monique M.B. Breteler,Aad van der Lugt +8 moreErasmus University Rotterdam

- 01 Nov 2007

- The New England Journal of Medicine

Show Less

TL;DR: The prevalence of asymptomatic brain infarcts and meningiomas increased with age, as did the volume of white-matter lesions, whereas aneurysms showed no age-related increase in prevalence.

TL;DR: The height differential between the tallest and shortest populations was 19-20 cm a century ago, and has remained the same for women and increased for men a century later despite substantial changes in the ranking of countries.
